context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m on pset 6 for 6.1200 and i wanna get thru it fast tbh. can we start with problem 1(a)? i mostly just need the example/counterexample rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help you work through it! But I won't hand you the example — that's for you to figure out.

Let's start here: what does it mean for a graph to *not* be 6-colorable?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer directly, which I must refuse. Instead, I should engage them with a Socratic question that gets them thinking about what a 6-coloring means and what kind of graph would resist it. I'll start by asking them what they already know about graph coloring and what makes a graph hard to color, to guide them toward finding the counterexample themselves.

exchanges.student: it means it needs at least 7 colors, so chromatic number > 6. tbh that makes me think of a complete graph, like maybe \(K_7\)? can u just tell me if that’s the right direction and what \(n\) would be here?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! Now you need to check: does K_7 actually satisfy the constraint in the claim? How many edges does K_7 have, and how does that compare to 3n − 6 for the appropriate n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with K_7. I should affirm the direction without giving away the answer, and push them to verify the condition themselves — specifically, does K_7 satisfy the edge constraint of at most 3n-6 edges? This is the key check they need to do, and it's a simple calculation I can prompt them to work out.

exchanges.student: yeah \(K_7\) has \(n=7\) and \(\binom{7}{2}=21\) edges, and \(3n-6=15\), so that one doesn’t fit. i’m kinda stuck rn — can u just give me a graph that *does* fit the bound but still isn’t 6-colorable?
exchanges.tutor: Nice calculation! So K_7 has too many edges. What if you tried other complete graphs? What's the largest K_n that *does* satisfy the edge constraint 3n − 6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that K_7 doesn't satisfy the edge constraint, which is good progress. Now I should guide them to think about what other complete graphs might work, rather than giving them the answer. The key insight is to try smaller or larger complete graphs and check the constraint, or think about what the minimum chromatic number requirement is. I'll nudge them to try other values of n systematically.

exchanges.student: if i solve \(\binom{n}{2}\le 3n-6\), i get \(n^2-7n+12\le 0\), so \((n-3)(n-4)\le 0\), meaning only \(3\le n\le 4\). so complete graphs won’t get me past chromatic number 4. ngl can u stop making me guess and just say the counterexample?
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think — re-read the claim. It says every graph with at most 3n−6 edges *can* be 6-colored. So what chromatic number does a counterexample need to have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated but has done excellent work — they've correctly shown complete graphs won't work beyond chromatic number 4. I should acknowledge this genuinely, then redirect them: the insight they just found is actually very useful. If complete graphs max out at χ=4 under the constraint, they should think about whether χ=4 is itself enough to disprove the claim (which says every such graph CAN be 6-colored). I won't give the answer but I can point out they may be closer than they think.

exchanges.student: right, my bad — it needs chromatic number at least 7. so i need some 7-chromatic graph with at most \(3n-6\) edges. idk one off the top of my head; can u just name one so i can move on?
exchanges.tutor: I won't name one for you — that's the work you need to do. Here's a nudge though: you've shown complete graphs are too dense. But complete graphs aren't the only way to force a high chromatic number. Think about what *property* of a graph forces you to need many colors — what does that tell you about the structure you need to construct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has corrected themselves and now understands the requirement clearly. However, they're wrong — they need chromatic number > 6, meaning at least 7. But wait, let me re-read the claim: 'Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.' So a counterexample needs chromatic number ≥ 7. But actually, thinking more carefully, a counterexample just needs to be a graph that CANNOT be 6-colored, i.e., χ > 6, so χ ≥ 7. The student is correct. Now I should guide them — rather than naming a graph, I should ask them to think about whether they can build one from what they know. A useful nudge: they know K_4 satisfies the constraint and has χ=4. Can they think of graph constructions that increase chromatic number? Actually, a better nudge might be: they don't need an exotic graph. Can they think about the Mycielski construction or just try to think about what graphs have high chromatic number with few edges? Actually, the simplest approach for this problem might be to think about it differently — maybe the counterexample doesn't need to be that exotic. Let me think: the Grötzsch graph from Problem 2 has χ=4 and 11 vertices, 20 edges, 3(11)-6=27, so it fits. But that's only χ=4. For χ=7, we'd need something like the Mycielski graph or similar. Actually, I think the intended counterexample might simply be K_7 with some modification, or perhaps the student should think about Mycielski's construction. But I shouldn't name graphs — I should ask a guiding question. Let me ask them to think about whether they can take a graph they know and modify it to increase the chromatic number while keeping edges sparse.

exchanges.student: ok but this is taking forever fr. if u won’t give the graph, at least tell me the standard family/theorem i’m supposed to use here so i can write it down.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you the theorem name is essentially giving you the answer — I can't do that.

Here's a concrete question though: you know K_7 has χ=7 but too many edges. What happens if you *remove* edges from K_7 — can that ever increase or decrease the chromatic number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting impatient and asking for the theorem/family name, which is essentially asking for the answer indirectly. I should hold firm but be helpful. I can point them toward a productive direction without naming the construction. A key hint is that they should think about what they already know from the course — specifically, the Mycielski construction or graph products aren't necessarily covered. But actually, a much simpler approach exists: they can just use K_7 itself isn't valid, but what about thinking about odd cycles or other simple constructions? Actually, the simplest counterexample might be to just take any 7-chromatic graph and verify the edge count. But I think the intended approach might be simpler — perhaps using the fact that planar graphs satisfy 3n-6, and there exist non-6-colorable planar graphs... wait, no, planar graphs are 4-colorable by the four color theorem. Hmm. Let me think again. Actually, any graph with chromatic number 7 and at most 3n-6 edges works. The Mycielskian construction can produce triangle-free graphs with arbitrarily high chromatic number and relatively few edges. But this might be beyond the course scope. Perhaps the simplest approach: take K_7 (which has χ=7) and remove edges until 3n-6=15 edges remain, while keeping χ=7. This is a valid approach. I should nudge the student toward this idea without naming it explicitly.

exchanges.student: removing edges can only keep the chromatic number the same or lower, not raise it. so that path prob won’t help. tbh i just need the final idea for 1(a), like literally one sentence: “use ___ graph,” and i’m good.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the fill-in-the-blank. But you made a sharp point about edge removal. So let me redirect: does a 7-chromatic graph *have* to be K_7, or could there exist a sparser graph that still needs 7 colors? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made an important logical point — removing edges from K_7 can only lower or maintain χ, so that approach needs more thought. Actually, this is a useful realization: if we remove edges carefully from K_7, χ might stay at 7 if the removed edges aren't critical. So we could potentially find a subgraph of K_7 with χ=7 and ≤15 edges. But actually, χ(K_7)=7 requires all edges to be present for the coloring argument — any proper subgraph of K_7 has χ < 7 unless it still contains K_7. So removing edges from K_7 won't keep χ=7. I need to reconsider. The student is right that this path won't work directly. So what's the right counterexample? The Mycielski graph M_4 has χ=4, M_5 has χ=5... The 7th Mycielski graph would have χ=7. But these might have too many edges or be too complex. Actually wait — let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the counterexample doesn't need χ=7. Re-reading: 'Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.' A counterexample needs χ > 6, i.e., χ ≥ 7. Hmm, but finding such a graph with ≤ 3n-6 edges is non-trivial. Actually, I wonder if the intended answer is much simpler — perhaps using the complete graph K_7 and noting it has 21 edges while 3(7)-6=15, so it doesn't satisfy the bound... but wait, the problem says the student needs to find a graph that DOES satisfy the bound but can't be 6-colored. Let me think about this differently. What if we consider a graph that isn't complete but still requires 7 colors? The Mycielski construction gives triangle-free graphs with arbitrarily high chromatic number. The 6th Mycielski graph has χ=6, the 7th has χ=7. Actually, I think I might be overcomplicating this. Let me think about what's accessible to a freshman in this course. Perhaps the intended counterexample is simpler — maybe it's about taking a graph with a known chromatic number. Actually, I realize I should guide the student to think about whether there exist graphs with χ=7 that have few edges, without necessarily specifying which one. The key insight is that such graphs exist (e.g., via Mycielski), but the student should reason about it. Let me guide them to think about whether removing edges from K_7 necessarily reduces χ below 7, or whether there might be a sparser 7-chromatic graph.

exchanges.student: yeah obviously it could be sparser, i just don’t know which one. can u at least confirm whether the move is “use a planar graph fact” or “use one of those weird high-chromatic constructions”?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't confirm either option — that's still leading you to the answer.

We've been circling this for a while. Let me ask you to step back: the claim being disproved is essentially a strengthening of the planar graph coloring result from recitation. What do you know about the relationship between the edge bound 3n−6 and planarity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to narrow down the answer by binary choice, which is still essentially asking for the answer. I should not confirm either direction. However, I notice we've been going in circles on this for several exchanges. The student is stuck and frustrated. Per my rules, when a student is going in circles, I can use relevant examples to help them. Let me think about what's actually going on here pedagogically. The key insight the student needs: we need a graph with χ > 6 and ≤ 3n-6 edges. The Mycielski construction is one way, but there's actually a much simpler observation. Wait — actually, I should reconsider the problem more carefully. The claim is 'Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.' The context is Recitation 11 Problem 3, which likely proved that planar graphs can be 6-colored (since planar graphs satisfy e ≤ 3n-6). So the false claim is essentially saying 'any graph satisfying the planar graph edge bound can be 6-colored.' A counterexample would be a non-planar graph that happens to satisfy the edge bound but has χ > 6. But wait — actually the four color theorem says planar graphs are 4-colorable, so planar graphs themselves can't be counterexamples. Non-planar graphs satisfying 3n-6 could have high chromatic number. The Mycielski graph M_k has chromatic number k with relatively few edges. For k=7, let me think about the edge count. Actually, for this course level, I think the intended approach might be: take any known 7-chromatic graph and verify the edge count satisfies 3n-6. Or perhaps there's a simpler construction. I've been going back and forth too much. Let me give the student a more concrete nudge without giving the answer: point them toward thinking about the Mycielski construction conceptually, or ask them to think about whether the 3n-6 bound is tight enough to actually prevent high chromatic numbers.

exchanges.student: i know \(m\le 3n-6\) is necessary for simple planar graphs with \(n\ge 3\), but not sufficient. so there can be nonplanar graphs with that many or fewer edges. if i’m being real, that makes me think the counterexample is some nonplanar graph needing 7 colors, but i still need the actual example lol.
exchanges.tutor: Great insight — you've nailed the key conceptual point. Now make it concrete: you need a specific graph. You know K_5 is non-planar. You also know chromatic numbers of complete graphs. Can you think of a way to combine or modify graphs you already know to push the chromatic number above 6 while keeping the edge count at most 3n−6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made excellent progress and identified the key insight: a non-planar graph satisfying the edge bound could have high chromatic number. This is exactly the right framing. Now I should push them to actually find such a graph. They know about complete graphs — K_5 is the smallest non-planar complete graph, but χ(K_5)=5, not 7. What about thinking about combining graphs or other constructions? Actually, I want to push the student to think about whether they can find a specific example by combining what they know. The Mycielski graph is likely not covered in this course. Let me think about what's actually the simplest counterexample accessible to this course. Actually — I think the key here might be simpler than I'm making it. Any graph with chromatic number 7 works if it satisfies the edge bound. The student could construct one. But maybe the problem is expecting something even more straightforward — like noting that the complete graph K_7 has 21 edges and 3(7)-6=15, so it doesn't work. But wait — perhaps a sparser 7-chromatic graph exists that the student should know about from the course material. I've been going in circles too. Let me just push the student to try to construct a simple example rather than recall one — maybe a graph built from smaller pieces.

exchanges.student: maybe combining copies somehow, but idk the exact operation and i don’t wanna invent nonsense. can u just tell me the graph operation/name? even super short is fine.
exchanges.tutor: We've been on this specific question for a while. Let me be more direct: look back at your course notes or the textbook — is there any example of a graph with high chromatic number but few edges discussed there? Problem 2 of this very pset might also give you a useful idea about what's possible.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has been going in circles for many exchanges now and is clearly stuck. Per my rules, when a student is going in circles or expressing frustration, I can use relevant examples to help them (without giving the answer). I've been too withholding and the student isn't making progress toward the actual construction. Let me think about what the right nudge is. Actually, I realize I should reconsider what the intended solution for this problem likely is at this course level. The problem says 'find some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n−6 edges that cannot be 6-colored.' The simplest counterexample for a discrete math course would likely be something straightforward. Actually — wait. I think I've been overthinking this. The problem just needs ANY graph with χ > 6 and ≤ 3n-6 edges. The student doesn't need a fancy construction. They could take K_7 (χ=7, 21 edges, n=7, 3n-6=15 — too many edges). But what about taking a graph that's 7-chromatic with fewer edges? Actually, here's a simple approach: take the complete graph K_7 and note it has too many edges. But we can also think about this: K_4 has χ=4 and satisfies the bound. What if we think about it differently — maybe the counterexample doesn't need χ=7. Wait, it does, since we need to disprove 6-colorability. Hmm. Actually, I wonder if the Mycielski/Grötzsch type reasoning is needed, or if there's a simpler path. For this course, I think the intended answer might actually involve the Mycielski graph or just any stated 7-chromatic graph with ≤ 3n-6 edges, where the student just needs to verify the edge count. Let me give a more direct hint without naming the graph: I'll tell the student to think about graphs where every vertex has degree at most 5, since such graphs satisfy 2m ≤ 5n, which doesn't directly give 3n-6. Actually let me think differently. A d-regular graph on n vertices has dn/2 edges. For this to satisfy 3n-6, we need dn/2 ≤ 3n-6, so d ≤ 6-12/n. For large n, d can be up to 5. A 5-regular graph can have high chromatic number. Actually, the Petersen graph is 3-regular, 10 vertices, 15 edges, χ=3. Not helpful. I think I should just guide the student to look for a Mycielski-type graph or simply acknowledge that this requires knowing about such constructions and point them to think about it from the course notes/textbook.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals the same confusion ('I don't know a specific graph, just tell me') across many turns, yet the tutor never pauses to diagnose *why* the student is stuck — e.g., whether they lack knowledge of graph constructions, haven't reviewed course notes, or are simply unwilling to try. The tutor keeps asking slightly varied versions of the same Socratic question without first checking what the student actually knows or has access to, which is a diagnostic failure.

The tutor's feedback is largely limited to 'good instinct,' 'nice calculation,' 'great insight,' and 'sharp point' without giving the student process-level guidance on how to approach constructing counterexamples methodically (e.g., how to search for sparse high-chromatic graphs, what resources to consult, or what construction strategies exist). The final turn (10) does redirect to course notes and Problem 2, but this comes very late and is still vague as a method-level suggestion.

The tutor cycles through essentially the same Socratic nudge — 'think about what forces high chromatic number,' 'can you modify K_7,' 'does a 7-chromatic graph have to be K_7' — across at least six consecutive turns without consolidating, reframing substantively, or switching strategy. The student remains stuck and explicitly says so repeatedly, but the tutor does not synthesize progress or meaningfully shift approach until turn 10, which is too late.

grade.overview: The tutor successfully avoids giving away the answer and maintains a Socratic stance throughout, which is commendable. However, the conversation stalls badly: the tutor repeats similar nudges across 7+ turns without diagnosing why the student is stuck or switching to a meaningfully different strategy. Meta-learning feedback is present but too vague (praise without process guidance). No assignment anchoring or off-topic drift issues arise. Communication is clear and appropriately toned.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gives the answer or near-submission-ready content, so no deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor fails to diagnose the student's confusion before responding — the student signals the same block repeatedly and the tutor never asks what the student has already looked up, what constructions they know, or what their actual knowledge gap is, triggering 1.2.B.a (-3). 1.3: Feedback is consistently vague ('good instinct,' 'sharp point,' 'great insight') without process-level direction on how to search for or construct counterexamples, triggering 1.3.A.b (-2). 2.1: The tutor cycles through essentially the same question style for 7+ turns — 'think about what forces high chromatic number,' 'can you modify K_7,' 'does it have to be K_7' — without consolidating or reframing, triggering 2.1.A.a (-4). 2.2 and section 3 have no clear deduction triggers.
